Card 1 of 15 · 3 U's Diagnostic
3 U's Diagnostic
LINKEDIN CAPTION
You have a personnel issue in front of you.

Before you decide what to do — diagnose.

The 3 U's Diagnostic is the TPS framework that separates 3/5 candidates from 4/5 candidates in Supervisory Judgment scenarios.

→ UNAWARE: They don't know it's a problem. Communicate. Not discipline.
→ UNABLE: They know but lack skill or resources. Coach. Not discipline.
→ UNWILLING: They know, they can, they choose not to. The ONLY situation where discipline applies.

Most candidates jump to action without diagnosing. Assessors notice. They write it on the rubric.

Your first words in any personnel scenario: "Before I decide how to respond, I need to determine which U this is."

That one sentence moves you from 3/5 to 4/5.

Save this for your next prep session.

📌 Module 1 is free at thepromotionalstandard.com — no account required.

Card 2 of 15 · Glasl Escalation Ladder
Glasl Escalation Ladder
LINKEDIN CAPTION
Not all conflict is the same. Using the wrong intervention for the wrong phase doesn't just fail — it escalates the conflict.

The Glasl Escalation Ladder is the TPS conflict framework that every assessor expects you to know.

Phase 1 (Win/Win): Both parties believe resolution is possible. Your role is mediator.
Phase 2 (Win/Lose): Positions are hardened. Coalition forming. Your role is authority.
Phase 3 (Lose/Lose): Destructive. Parties willing to harm themselves to harm the other. Escalate above rank.

The most common candidate error: applying Phase 2 tools to a Phase 1 conflict.

It feels decisive. Assessors score it as overreach.

Identify the phase. Match the intervention. Name it out loud.

📌 Module 1 free at thepromotionalstandard.com

Card 3 of 15 · STAR-P Breakdown
STAR-P Breakdown
LINKEDIN CAPTION
Every assessor panel in America is scoring your oral board response against the same structure.

STAR-P. And most candidates are failing it without knowing why.

S — Situation: 15%
T — Task: 10%
A — Action: 50% ← This is where assessors score you
R — Result: 15%
P — Principle: 10% ← Named TPS framework

The mistake: candidates spend 35-40% on Situation telling the story. Assessors aren't scoring the story. They're scoring the Action.

Open specifically. Invest 50% in Action. Close with a named principle.

Save this for your next prep session.

📌 Module 1 free at thepromotionalstandard.com

Card 4 of 15 · The 50% Rule
The 50% Rule
LINKEDIN CAPTION
Here is the single most important oral board rule that no one talks about.

50% of your response time belongs to the Action section.

Not 30%. Not 40%. Fifty percent.

Because assessors score behavior. And behavior lives in Action.

A 3/5 candidate spends 40% of their time telling the story and 25% in Action.
A 4/5 candidate flips that — 15% Situation, 50% Action.

Same content. Completely different score.

After your next practice session — estimate your Action percentage. Most candidates are shocked.

Card 5 of 15 · T·C·H·O·E·D·S
T·C·H·O·E·D·S
LINKEDIN CAPTION
Seven principles. One leadership philosophy.

T·C·H·O·E·D·S is the TPS framework assessors score against in Ethics, Leadership Philosophy, and Personnel Issue scenarios.

T — Trust: Built through consistency, not words.
C — Courage: The willingness to have the hard conversation.
H — Humility: The willingness to be wrong and learn.
O — Ownership: I for failures. WE for achievements.
E — Empathy: Understanding the experience of those you lead.
D — Dedication: Work ethic and commitment to the mission.
S — Service: Leadership as identity — not position.

The 3/5 candidate says "I believe in these values."
The 5/5 candidate says "This situation tests the Courage element of T·C·H·O·E·D·S — here is why."

Name the element. Connect it to the scenario. That's the move.

📌 Module 1 free at thepromotionalstandard.com

Card 6 of 15 · Bank of Leadership
Bank of Leadership
LINKEDIN CAPTION
Every leadership decision you make is either a deposit or a withdrawal.

The Bank of Leadership is the TPS framework for understanding why followership is earned — not granted by rank.

Deposits build the account: keeping your word, advocating for your crew, taking accountability, developing people.

Withdrawals drain it: breaking commitments, taking credit, avoiding hard conversations, inconsistent standards.

The rule: you cannot make withdrawals from an empty account.

In assessment center scenarios your closing principle isn't just about solving the immediate problem. It's about what this decision deposits or withdraws from the trust account that makes everything else possible.

"How I handle this is a deposit or withdrawal from the account that allows me to lead under pressure."

That's a 5/5 close.

📌 thepromotionalstandard.com — Module 1 free

Card 7 of 15 · Skill/Will Matrix
Skill/Will Matrix
LINKEDIN CAPTION
You have a crew member underperforming. Before you decide what to do — where are they on the matrix?

High Skill / High Will → Delegate. Trust their judgment. Do not micromanage.
High Skill / Low Will → Investigate. Capable but choosing not to perform. Why?
Low Skill / High Will → Coach. Willing but not yet capable. Invest.
Low Skill / Low Will → Structure. Close supervision, clear expectations, documentation.

The mistake: applying the same leadership response to all four quadrants.

Name the quadrant. Name the response. Connect it to the 3 U's.

That's the diagnostic sequence assessors are looking for.

Card 8 of 15 · 1-5 Scoring Scale
1-5 Scoring Scale
LINKEDIN CAPTION
Most candidates prepare to pass an assessment center. The ones who promote prepare to score above a 3.

Here's what assessors are actually writing on the rubric:

1/5 — "Would cause harm at this rank."
2/5 — "Not ready for this level of responsibility."
3/5 — "Competent. Does not demonstrate [rank] leadership identity."
4/5 — "Strong candidate. Names frameworks. Closes with leadership principle."
5/5 — "Already performing at [rank] level. Clear promotion recommendation."

The difference between 3 and 4 is preparation depth.
The difference between 4 and 5 is identity.

Card 9 of 15 · Nine Question Buckets
Nine Question Buckets
LINKEDIN CAPTION
Every question in every assessment center falls into one of nine buckets.

Most candidates don't know the buckets. They walk in responding to what the question sounds like — instead of what assessors are actually scoring.

01 — Personnel Issue → 3 U's Diagnostic
02 — Conflict Resolution → Glasl Escalation Ladder
03 — Ethics and Integrity → T·C·H·O·E·D·S Trust
04 — Leadership Philosophy → T·C·H·O·E·D·S + Identity
05 — Change Management → Heifetz Adaptive vs Technical
06 — Development + Mentoring → 70-20-10 + SBI Feedback
07 — Strategic + Organizational → SWOT / OKRs / RACI
08 — Communication + Interpersonal → STAR-P + Bias check
09 — In-Basket + Priority → Eisenhower Matrix + Pareto

Before every rep — identify the bucket. Name the primary framework. Know what assessors are scoring.

Card 10 of 15 · Technical vs Adaptive
Technical vs Adaptive
LINKEDIN CAPTION
The most common and costly mistake in organizational leadership:

Applying a technical solution to an adaptive challenge.

Technical challenges have known solutions. Expertise solves them.

Adaptive challenges require new learning. They cannot be solved with the expertise that got you here. People must give up something they value.

The diagnostic test: "Does solving this require anyone to give up something they value?"
If yes — it is adaptive. Technical solutions won't hold.

A 3/5 candidate proposes policy, training, and discipline.
A 5/5 candidate says: "This is an adaptive challenge. The technical solution has been tried. The real work is understanding what people would have to give up."

📌 Module 1 free at thepromotionalstandard.com

Card 11 of 15 · CLEAR Feedback
CLEAR Feedback
LINKEDIN CAPTION
Most feedback in fire service sounds like this:
"Good job, but you could improve [vague area]."

That's not feedback. That's a report card.

CLEAR feedback is the TPS framework for feedback that actually develops people.

C — COMMEND: Specific behavioral evidence. Not encouragement.
L — LIMITATIONS: The single most important gap — named behaviorally.
E — EXAMPLES: Model excellence out loud. Don't describe it. Show it.
A — ADJUST: One change for the next rep. Not three. One.
R — REPEAT: Run the rep again immediately. Development happens in the correction.

The REPEAT is the step most supervisors skip.

The development happens in the next rep — not in the reflection.

📌 thepromotionalstandard.com — Module 1 free

#FireLeadership #FireService #Coaching #SupervisoryDevelopment #AssessmentCenter

Card 12 of 15 · Five-Paragraph Formula
Five-Paragraph Formula
LINKEDIN CAPTION
Every written exercise. Every rank. Every department.

The Five-Paragraph Formula is what assessors are scoring.

P1 — PURPOSE STATEMENT: Why does this document exist? One sentence. Most commonly missed. No purpose = cannot score above 3/5.

P2 — BACKGROUND: Relevant facts only. 3-5 sentences. Not a story.

P3 — ANALYSIS: Your longest paragraph. What does this mean? This is where supervisory thinking is visible.

P4 — RECOMMENDATIONS: "I recommend X by [date] because [rationale]." A decision — not a suggestion.

P5 — CLOSE AND FOLLOW-UP: Never skip this. Even if time is short.

Timed protocol: 5 min read → 3 min outline → 22 min draft → 5 min review.

📌 Module 1 free at thepromotionalstandard.com

#FireService #AssessmentCenter #WrittenExercise #PromotionalExam #FireLeadership

Card 13 of 15 · Eisenhower Matrix
Eisenhower Matrix
LINKEDIN CAPTION
You open the in-basket. Twelve items. 45 minutes. Where do you start?

The candidates who score 4/5 don't just triage instinctively. They triage explicitly — and name their logic out loud.

Q1 (Important + Urgent) → DO FIRST. Safety issues, crises, critical deadlines.
Q2 (Important + Not Urgent) → SCHEDULE. Strategic planning, development. Protect this time.
Q3 (Not Important + Urgent) → DELEGATE. Routine requests, interruptions. Name who.
Q4 (Not Important + Not Urgent) → ELIMINATE. Time wasters.

The move from 3/5 to 4/5:

"Using the Eisenhower Matrix — this item is Q1, Important and Urgent, my first action. This item is Q3 — I'm delegating to [name] because [rationale]."

Explicit triage logic scores 4. Intuitive triage scores 3.

📌 thepromotionalstandard.com — Module 1 free

Card 14 of 15 · James Clear Identity
James Clear Identity
LINKEDIN CAPTION
You don't score 4/5 by trying harder. You score 4/5 by becoming a different kind of thinker.

James Clear's identity architecture is the framework behind the TPS coaching methodology.

Outcome-based: "I want to promote." Sets goals. Hopes behavior follows. Fails under pressure.

Identity-based: "I am the kind of [rank] who diagnoses before acting, names frameworks explicitly, and closes with a leadership principle — because that is who I am."

Every practice rep casts a vote for that identity.
After enough votes — the behavior becomes automatic under pressure.

Exercise: Complete this sentence right now.
"I am the kind of [your target rank] who ___."

Write it down. Run your next rep from that identity.

📌 Module 1 free at thepromotionalstandard.com

Card 15 of 15 · The Four Dimensions
The Four Dimensions
LINKEDIN CAPTION
Every assessor. Every exercise. Every rank. The same four dimensions — every time.

01 — SUPERVISORY JUDGMENT: Most weighted. Diagnose with 3 U's before any action. Close with a named principle.

02 — INTERPERSONAL SKILLS: Not about being nice. About bias awareness, conflict navigation, and de-escalation with framework.

03 — ORAL COMMUNICATION: STAR-P structure. 50% in Action. Named closing principle. Open specifically.

04 — WRITTEN COMMUNICATION: Five-Paragraph Formula. Purpose statement in P1. Analysis in P3. Specific decisions in P4.

Before any practice session — review these four. Know what assessors are scoring in each dimension.

Most candidates prepare content. Top candidates prepare to demonstrate specific behavioral indicators in all four dimensions.

That's the difference between studying and developing.
